coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster nanosatellite for ratiometric fluorescence probe and imaging for hydrogen peroxide in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zenghe
Guo, Song
Yuan, Zhiqin
Lu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Developing probes for sensitive H2O2 detection is critical for understanding its related physiological and pathological processes. In this study, novel carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster (CQD-AuNC) nanosatellite with high fluorescence energy transfer efficiency (∼40%) and dual-emission fluorescent properties have been exploited for fluorescence ratiometric sensing of H2O2. The sensing mechanism was based on the fact that H2O2 can quench the red emission of AuNCs. Under the optimal conditions, the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite probe was sensitive to H2O2 in the range from 5.0nM to 80nM with a limit of detection of 2.9nM. The practicality of the proposed CQD-AuNC nanosatellite probe was validated by imaging H2O2 in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescence
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Intracellular imaging of H2O2 with CQD-AuNC nanosatellite
Synthesis and characterization of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between CQDs and AuNCs
Cytotoxicity of the proposed CQD-AuNC nanosatellite
Intracellular H2O2 imaging using CQD-AuNC nanosatellite nanoprobe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite-based probe for the detection of H2O2.

(A) HRTEM image of BPEI-CQDs; (B) HRTEM image of DHLA-AuNCs; (C) Absorption (blue) and fluorescence emission (red) spectra of BPEI-CQDs; (D) Absorption (blue) and fluorescence emission (red) spectra of and DHLA-AuNCs. Inset images are the corresponding digital photographs under UV light illumination. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) HRTEM image of the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite. (B) FT-IR spectra of the DHLA-AuNCs (1), BPEI-CQDs (2), and CQD-AuNC nanosatellite (3).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of as-prepared BPEI-CQDs (blue), DHLA-AuNCs (red), CQD-AuNC nanosatellite (green) and CQD-AuNC mixture (purple). (B) Spectral overlap between emission spectra of BPEI-CQDs (dotted blue) and absorption spectra of DHLA-AuNCs (red); The excitation wavelength was 380 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite solution in the presence of H2O2 at various concentrations upon excited at 380nm. Inset: photographs of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite solution after adding H2O2 with different concentrations under UV irradiation (from left to right: 0, 10−5, 10−3 and 10−1 M); (B) Plots of fluorescence intensity ratio (I 620/I 450) versus the concentration of H2O2.

Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells treated without (A) and with (B) 5μg/mL PMA stimulator for 4h after incubation with CQD-AuNC nanosatellite at 37°C for 5h (1–3: fluorescence images at blue channel, red channel and overlay of two channel). Scale bar, 40μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster nanosatellite for ratiometric fluorescence probe and imaging for hydrogen peroxide in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing probes for sensitive H2O2 detection is critical for understanding its related physiological and pathological processes. In this study, novel carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster (CQD-AuNC) nanosatellite with high fluorescence energy transfer efficiency (∼40%) and dual-emission fluorescent properties have been exploited for fluorescence ratiometric sensing of H2O2. The sensing mechanism was based on the fact that H2O2 can quench the red emission of AuNCs. Under the optimal conditions, the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite probe was sensitive to H2O2 in the range from 5.0nM to 80nM with a limit of detection of 2.9nM. The practicality of the proposed CQD-AuNC nanosatellite probe was validated by imaging H2O2 in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: There is continuing interest in the development of analyte detection methods based on fluorescence technology because of its high sensitivity, simple manipulation and admirable spatial and temporal resolution [1]. To achieve sensitive fluorescence detection, many organic fluorophores based chemosensors have been developed by modulating photoinduced electron transfer, internal charge transfer, or fluorescence energy transfer processes during the reaction with target analytes [2,3]. In addition to organic fluorophores, fluorescent nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and noble metal nanoclusters (NCs), have attracted increasing interest as a function of fluorescence probes for a variety of analytical applications and biological imaging [4–6]. Particularly, CQDs and NCs have been widely used for chem/biosensing, labeling and optical imaging in recent years due to their unique optical properties, large Stokes-shift and excellent biocompatibility [7–9]. They also show promising capability in cell imaging [10–12].

CQDs consist of sp2/sp3 carbon and oxygen/nitrogen-based groups were usually prepared via oxide cutting carbon resources or carbonization of organic molecules [13]. The quantum size effect and surface state/defect play important roles on the fluorescence of CQDs [14]. In comparison, due to the comparable size to the Fermi wavelength of electrons, AuNCs exhibit discrete electronic energy levels. They also possess strong light absorption through electronic transitions between molecule-like energy levels and usually result in the appearance of fluorescence [15,16]. It is generally accepted that the photophysical properties of AuNCs depend on their size, structure, oxidation state, and surface ligand [17–19]. So far, most of CQD or AuNC-based probes reply on the changes of single emission intensity during the detection of targets [20–23]. However, the interferences from other environmental parameters (e.g., pH, temperature, ionic strength, etc.) may affect their emission intensities and lead to false-positive and decreased sensitivity [24–27]. To circumvent these drawbacks, ratiometric probes, which simultaneously monitor fluorescence intensities of two isolated emission components, are being approvingly exploited because of their built-in correction characters [28–33]. For instance, Qu et al. developed disorderly assembled CQD-AuNC nanocomplexes for reliable recognition of reactive oxygen species (ROS) with a limit of detection of 0.5μM, where glutathione-protected AuNCs and cysteine-templated CQDs were embedded in one large particle via crosslinking reaction [34]. Despite the accuracy, the low sensitivity is still pending, which might attribute to the burial of partial AuNCs. It is generally accepted that ratiometric probes with core-shell structures provide high sensitivity because of large response area [35]. Therefore, developing fluorescent probes with core-shell structures would be an effective strategy to enhance the assay sensitivity.

Herein, we prepared carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster (CQD-AuNC) nanosatellite by assembly of dihydrolipoic acid-protected AuNCs (DHLA-AuNCs) onto branched poly(ethylenimine)-capped CQDs (BPEI-CQDs) surface via carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction using 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) as crosslinking agents. The prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite exhibited dual-emission fluorescent property with a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) efficiency of ∼40% between CQDs and AuNCs. In addition, the red-emission from DHLA-AuNCs can be quenched by H2O2, while blue-emission from BPEI-CQDs can act as the reference signal for providing built-in correction (Fig. 1 ). The proposed ratiometric fluorescence probe was sensitive to H2O2 in the range from 5.0nM to 80nM with a limit of detection of 2.9nM. Therefore, the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was applied for visual detection and intracellular imaging of H2O2 in living cells.

Citric acid (CA) and branched poly (ethylenimine) (BPEI) with molecular weight of 1800Da were obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), glutathione, ascorbic acid, cysteine, glucose and dopamine were purchased from J&K Chemical Ltd. (Beijing, China). 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were supplied by TCI, Inc. Lipoic acid (LA), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiahiazol-2-y1)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT assay kit) and phorbol myristate acetate (PMA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Other reagents and solvents were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). All chemicals used were of analytical grade without any further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2MΩ·cm), which was obtained through a Millipore Milli-Q water system (Barnstead, CA, USA).

The UV–vis spectra were carried out on a Shimadzu UV-2401PC spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and the slit widths were set to be 5.0nm for both the excitation and emission. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image was measured on a JEOL JEM-3010 microscope (Tokyo, Japan), which was operated at an accelerating voltage of 300kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed with a Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzer (Malvern, U.K.). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Thermo Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Madison, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo-VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer, using Al Kα X-ray radiation (1486.6eV) for excitation, and all binding energies were calibrated by C1s (284.6eV) as the reference energy. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured with an Edinburgh FLS 980 Lifetime and Steady State Spectrometer (Edinburgh, U.K.). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield was performed from the reconvolution fit analysis (Edinburgh F980 analysis software) on an Edinburgh instrument spectrometer with an integrating sphere unit. Confocal fluorescence imaging was obtained with a Leica TCS SP5 confocal laser-scanning microscope (Munich, Germany).

All glasswave was cleaned with freshly prepared aqua regia (concentrated HCl:HNO3, 3:1v/v) and thoroughly rinsed in ultrapure water prior to use. The BPEI-capped CQDs were prepared according to the previously reported procedure with slight modification [36]. Typically, CA (1.0g) and BPEI (0.5g) were dissolved uniformly with 10mL hot water in a 25mL beaker and followed by evaporation to form gel at 200°C in about 20min. Then, a 1.0mL of ultrapure water was added into the resulted pale-yellow gel before scorched and hydrothermal heating was continued. The color of the gel was turned to orange after the procedure was repeated about 10 times in 3h, indicating the formation of BPEI-CQDs. The as-prepared CQDs were then diluted to 10mL solution with ultrapure water and purified by the dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 3.5 KDa for 24h. The obtained BPEI-CQDs were stored under 4°C for further study.

DHLA-stabilized AuNCs (DHLA-AuNCs) were synthesized adopting the reported method with little modification [37]. In brief, 5.2mg LA was solubilized in 15.6mL of ultrapure water containing 40μL of NaOH (2.0M). After 15min of vigorous stirring, 160μL of 2wt% HAuCl4 was added into the previous solution, and the mixture was continued to stir for another 5min. Subsequently, 320μL of previously prepared ice cool NaBH4 (50mM) was added slowly under rapid stirring. After stirring overnight, the as-prepared AuNCs were dialyzed with dialysis tube (MWCO: 3.5kDa) for removing unwanted regents and stored at 4°C.

The CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was prpeared via carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction. Briefly, BPEI-CQD and DHLA-AuNC solutions with a volume ratio of 1:2 were mixed and then a solution of 10mg/mL EDC/NHS was added (EDC/NHS:BPEI-CQDs=1:1v/v). EDC and NHS were used to generate activated ester and form amido link between carboxyl moieties of DHLA-AuNCs and amine groups of BPEI-CQDs [38,39]. The mixture was stirred under room temperature in the dark for 3h. The resulting CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was then purified by dialysis tube (MWCO: 3.5kDa) and stored at 4°C for further experiments.

In a typical experiment, HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100U of penicillin, and 100μg/mL streptomycin at 37°C under 5% CO2. For the MTT toxicity test of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite, HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate in cell medium overnight in CO2 culture box. After the addition of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite with concentrations from 0.1 to 6.4mg/mL, 10μL of MTT solution (5mg/mL) was added to each well. Then, 100μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals after 4h of incubation. The absorbance at 570nm was measured using a Multimode Plate Readers after all the formazan crystals were dissolved. The cell viability (%) was calculated from [A]treated/[A]control ×100%, where [A]treated and [A]control are the absorbance values of the wells with and without CQD-AuNC nanosatellite, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging of H2O2 with CQD-AuNC nanosatellite
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were plated onto 35-mm Petri dishes and cultured for 24h in a humid atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37°C. After being incubated with CQD-AuNC nanosatellite (3.0mg/mL in culture medium) for 5h, the cells were stimulated with PMA (5μg/mL) at 37°C for another 5h. PMA was introduced to produce H2O2 [40]. The cells were then washed three times with PBS buffer before fluorescence imaging. Cell imaging was measured by a confocal fluorescence microscope with a 20×objective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite
ce:section.ce:para: Before the synthesis of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite, BPEI-CQDs and DHLA-AuNCs were firstly prepared via different approaches, respectively. The CQDs were prepared by direct pyrogenation of the mixture of CA and BPEI. As shown in Fig. 2 A, HRTEM image showed monodisperse BPEI-CQDs that possessed near spherical morphology with an average diameter of 5.0nm. The high magnificaiton image (inset of Fig. 2A) further revealed that the BPEI-CQDs had 0.212nm lattices spacing, which was consistent with typical (100) facet of graphite [41]. The strong blue emission from transparent BPEI-CQDs solution was easily observed under UV illumination (inset of Fig. 2C), suggesting the successful preparation of the fluorescent BPEI-CQDs. As can be seen from Fig. 2C, an evident emission peak of BPEI-CQDs around 450nm appeared under the 350nm excitation. On the other hand, the DHLA-AuNCs were synthesized via chemical reduction of gold salt. The average size and morphology of the obtained DHLA-AuNCs were measured by HRTEM. According to the HRTEM image (Fig. 2B), the as-prepared AuNCs also showed spherical morphology with an average diameter of about 2.0nm. Compared to BPEI-CQDs, the DHLA-AuNCs solution showed red emission under UV illumination (inset of Fig. 2D). Correspondingly, the fluorescence spectra of red-emitting DHLA-AuNCs solution revealed a peak with emission maximum of 620nm under 550nm excitation (Fig. 2D).

The CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was synthesized via the coupling reaction between amino groups of BPEI-CQDs and carboxyl groups of DHLA-AuNCs by using EDC/NHS as crosslinking agents. The resulting CQD-AuNC nanosatellite solution showed the peachblow emission upon exposure to UV light. To verify the successful conjugation between BPEI-CQDs and DHLA-AuNCs, HRTEM and FT-IR measurements were performed. From the HRTEM image (Fig. 3 A) of the obtained CQD-AuNC nanosatellite, nanostructures comprised some DHLA-AuNCs around the BPEI-CQDs were observed, indicating the formation of amido link between BPEI-CQDs and DHLA-AuNCs. It can be seen from Fig. 3A, five or six AuNCs are bound onto CQDs surface. However, the AuNCs number on CQDs would be not absolutely equal because of the randomicity of nanochemistry. Therefore, a schematic illustration of six AuNCs on CQDs was used to stand for CQD-AuNC nanosatellite (Fig. 1). As shown in Fig. 3A, the distance between CQD and AuNC is less than 2nm. The distance was calculated to be 1.4±0.4nm according to HRTEM images. Since the length of LA is close to 1nm, such a short distance suggested that AuNCs are linked onto CQDs surface. Further evidence for the formation of nanosatellite structure was supported by DLS measurements (Fig. S1). The hydrodynamic diameter of CQDs changed from 8.5 to 20.7nm after crosslinking with AuNCs. The slight increase of hydrodynamic diameter ruled out the formation of large CQD-AuNC aggregates similar to Qu’s report [34]. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 3B, the FT-IR spectrum of amino-functionalized CQDs exhibited broad band at 3459cm−1 and the sharp band at 1570cm−1, which could be attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH [36]. The DHLA-protected AuNCs showed stretching vibrations of OH at 3410cm−1, CO stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups at 1720cm−1. For the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite, an increase in absorption band at 1650cm−1 was assigned to the CO stretching vibrations of acylamide of the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite, further indicating the formation of amido link via carbodiimide-activated coupling [32,34]. Therefore, CQD-Au NC nanosatellite structure was formed. The fluorescence spectra of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite solution at different concentrations of NaCl and pH values were measured (Fig. S2). It was seen that the addition of NaCl even with high concentration showed slight influence on the fluorescence of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite. While the solution pH affected the fluorescence indeed, and maximum fluorescence around pH 6.0 was appeared. However, the fluorescence at pH 7.0 was comparable to that of pH 6.0. Thus, CQD-AuNC nanosatellite might be usable under biological media with a pH of 7.4 and ∼137mM NaCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between CQDs and AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: It was seen that the red emission (620nm) of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was amplified in comparison to the sole DHLA-AuNCs (Fig. 4 A) and a decreased blue emission (450nm) exhibited simultaneously, while direct mixing of PEI-CQDs and DHLA-AuNCs didn’t show such a similar fluorescence change, suggesting the FRET between BPEI-CQDs (donor) and DHLA-AuNCs (acceptor) may occur, which enhances the red emission of acceptor. Such a hypothesis was proved by following four facts. First, as shown in Fig. 4B, the blue-emitting BPEI-CQDs showed fluorescence spectra in the range of 420–550nm and the red-emitting DHLA-AuNCs exhibited absorption spectra with a weak shoulder around 505nm, indicating the excellent spectral overlap between BPEI-CQDs emission and DHLA-AuNCs absorption and the occurrence of FRET [42]. Second, the formation of amido link shortened the distance between the BPEI-CQDs and DHLA-AuNCs and the distance was calculated to be 1.4nm (less than 10nm) from above HRTEM results, further facilitating the accomplishing of FRET. Third, during the formation of nanosatellite structure, the fluorescence lifetime of PEI-CQDs (donor) displayed obvious decrease (see discussion below). Last, the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite and DHLA-AuNCs showed red emission quantum yields (QYs) of 9.3% and 6.9%, respectively. All those results indicated the occurrence of FRET between BPEI-CQDs and DHLA-AuNCs.

The formation of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite might be affected by several factors, including the concentration of the BPEI-CQDs and reaction time (Fig. S3). A systematic survey was performed to optimize these experimental parameters. The strongest red emission around 620nm after the coupling reaction was achieved when the BPEI-CQDs concentration was 0.15mg/mL and the reaction time was 18h. Fig. S4 showed the fluorescence decay profiles of BPEI-CQDs, DHLA-AuNCs and CQD-AuNC nanosatellite. The blue emission lifetimes of BPEI-CQDs and CQD-AuNC nanosatellite were 9.5ns and 5.8ns, respectively. While the red emission lifetimes of DHLA-AuNCs and CQD-AuNC nanosatellite were 1.9μs and 2.1μs, revealing the changes of chemical environment around the AuNCs [43]. The FRET efficiency was calculated to be ∼40% based on the fluorescence lifetime measurements (Table S1) [44]. The high FRET efficiency might be attributed to the favorable overlap of spectra and short distance between CQD donor and AuNC acceptor. Therefore, all subsequent experiments involved in CQD-AuNC nanosatellite were conducted at this condition.

The bright fluorescence with the high FRET efficiency makes the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite as a promising probe for fluorimetric sensing. In this study, the fluorescence emission spectra of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite solution were recorded after adding H2O2 with different concentrations. The CQD-AuNC nanosatellite presented the double emission peaks centered at 450 and 620nm under the 380nm excitation, which are ascribed to blue-emitting BPEI-CQDs and red-emitting DHLA-AuNCs, respectively. The fluorescence intensity at 620nm was gradually quenched when the H2O2 concentration was increased (Fig. 5 A). In addition to the spectral changes, the variation of fluorescence color of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite solution from peachblow to blue was easily observed under UV irradiation upon increasing the concentrations of H2O2 (inset of Fig. 5A). These phenomena revealed that the fluorescence of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was sensitive to H2O2. Fig. 5B displayed that the intensity ratio of red and blue emission (I 620/I 450) showed a good linear relationship versus the H2O2 concentration ranging from 5.0nM to 80nM. The corresponding regression equation of the working curve was easily described as: Y =−0.0043 X +1.5965 (R2 =0.9811), where Y was the intensity ratio (I 620/I 450) and X was the concentration of H2O2. Moreover, the limit of detection toward H2O2 with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was estimated to be ∼2.9nM, which is much lower than that of sole AuNCs (0.21μM) [45] or CQD-AuNC nanocomplexes (0.5μM) [34] probes. The possible reasons for favorable sensitivity might be assigned to the nanosatellite structure and high FRET efficiency of proposed probe [44]. The fluorescence quenching of AuNCs was ascribed to high oxidation capacity of H2O2. It is generally accepted that ligand to metal (S→Au) charge transfer plays an important role on the fluorescence of thiolate capped AuNCs. As indicated in previous reports [46,47], the addition of H2O2 can induce the oxidation of AuNCs, which liberates thiolate from the AuNCs surface and inhibits the ligand to metal charge transfer process, subsequently diminishes the fluorescence. To verify this hypothesis, the XPS spectra of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite without and with the addition of 1.0mM of H2O2 were measured (Fig. S5). The XPS spectra showed the Au 4f7/2 of the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was deconvoluted into two distinct components at 84.0 and 85.1eV, corresponding to Au(0) and Au(I), indicating the coexistence of Au(0) and Au(I) in the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite [48]. It was clearly seen that an evident increase in the percentage of Au(I) in the AuNCs appeared after the addition of H2O2. The percentage of Au+ without H2O2 was calculated to be ∼12% by fitting the Au 4f XPS spectrum with two components, while it became ∼83% after adding 1mM H2O2, indicating the oxidation of AuNCs. These results revealed that the fluorescence quenching behavior was attributable to the oxidation of the AuNCs by H2O2. The selectivity of the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite toward H2O2 was tested. Under the same condition (PBS buffer), the fluorescence spectra of CQD-AuNC nanosatellite upon addition of H2O2 (1μM), and some other small molecules (20μM) and metal ions (1mM) were recorded. As manifested in Fig. S6, none of these small molecules or metal ions could cause a conspicuous fluorescence quenching as H2O2 (1μM) did. Therefore, a sensitive and selective H2O2 nanoprobe can be fabricated based on fluorescent CQD-AuNC nanosatellite via a facile approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of the proposed CQD-AuNC nanosatellite
ce:section.ce:para: For further biological application, the cytotoxicity is a major factor in the development of an intracellular sensor system. Herein, the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite toward HeLa cancer cells was determined through a standard MTT assay. The degree of toxicity of the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite probe was determined by incubating HeLa cells with the as-prepared nanosatellite with the different concentrations for 12h. As demonstrated in Fig. S7, the cell viability was decreased only by ∼20% for a CQD-AuNC nanosatellite concentration of 0.4mg/mL after 12h incubation. A further increase in the concentration of the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite led to the lower cell viability, suggesting the toxicity of the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was proportional to their concentrations. These results might be attributed to the fact that both AuNCs and CQDs showed increased cytotoxicity at extremely high concentrations [49,50]. Nevertheless, the HeLa cells maintained favorable viability (>75%) even with high concentration (3.2mg/mL) of the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite cell viability after incubation for 12h. These results demonstrated that the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite was generally biocompatible and suitable for intracellular H2O2 imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular H2O2 imaging using CQD-AuNC nanosatellite nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: After the investigation of in vitro analytical performance of the CQD-AuNC nanosatellite-based ratiometric fluorescent probe, we explored its capability for intracellular H2O2 imaging. HeLa cells were used as the model system. In this experiment, HeLa cells were firstly incubated with the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite probe in cell culture media at 37°C for 5h. It was clearly showed that the as-prepared CQD-AuNC nanosatellite had excellent cell permeability, and both blue and red fluorescence after incubation was observed (Fig. 6 A). Previous studies have showed that exposure to PMA could promote the production of H2O2 in macrophage cells [40]. The intracellular H2O2 was generated upon the addition of 5.0μg/mL PMA stimulator. After PMA stimulation, the red fluorescence was greatly decreased while blue fluorescence was largely preserved (Fig. 6B). This indicated that the AuNCs from CQD-AuNC nanosatellite could be efficiently quenched under conditions relevant to inflammation, providing clear evidence of fluorescence quenching-based ratiometric imaging of H2O2 in living cells. In addition, the overlapped images exhibited dramatic difference without and with PMA stimulation. All the above results confirmed that intracellular H2O2 could quench the fluorescence signal at the red channel, revealing the practicability of CQD-AuNC nansatellite probe for imaging H2O2 in living cells.

In summary, a FRET-based nanosatellite probe has been designed for ratiometric visual detection and intracellular imaging of H2O2 through assembly of red-emissive DHLA-AuNCs onto the surface of blue-emissive BPEI-CQDs through carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction. The resulted CQD-AuNC nanosatellite displayed a dual-emission property, preventing the proposed rariometric fluorescence probe from environmental interferences because of the inherent built-in correction characters. On the basis of H2O2-induced oxidation of DHLA-AuNCs, the red emission was greatly quenched by H2O2, consequently bringing about the distinct changes of fluorescence color from peachblow to purple, and finally blue. The intensity ratio of red and blue emission showed a good linear relationship versus the H2O2 concentration. Moreover, intracellular imaging of H2O2 could be achieved using the proposed nanosatellite probe.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, 2014CB932103), the National Natural Foundation of China (21656001, 21575010, 21375006 and 21605003), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, and Innovation and Promotion Project of Beijing University of Chemical Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.10.134.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Imaging fluorescence fluctuation spectroscopy: new tools for quantitative bioimaging
Fluorescent and luminescent probes for detection of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Chemistry, biology, and medicine of fluorescent nanomaterials and related systems: new insights into biosensing, bioimaging, genomics, diagnostics, and therapy
Fluorescent silver nanoclusters stabilized by DNA scaffolds
Synthesis and unique photoluminescence properties of nitrogen-rich quantum dots and their applications
Fluorescent gold nanoclusters: recent advances in sensing and imaging
Fluorescent gold nanodots based sensor array for proteins discrimination
One-step green synthesis of fluorescent bimetallic Au/Ag nanoclusters for temperature sensing and in vitro detection of Fe3+
Carbon dot-based inorganic–organic nanosystem for two-photon imaging and biosensing of pH variation in living cells and tissues
Engineered CpG-antigen conjugates protected gold nanoclusters as smart self-vaccines for enhanced immune response and cell imaging
Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups
The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective
Functionalization of metal nanoclusters for biomedical applications
Atomically precise metal nanoclusters: stable sizes and optical properties
Luminescent metal nanoclusters with aggregation-induced emission
Identification of a highly luminescent Au22(SG)18 nanocluster
Functionalized fluorescent gold nanodots: synthesis and application for Pb2+ sensing
Functionalized gold nanoclusters as fluorescent labels for immunoassays: application to human serum immunoglobulin E determination
A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots
Selective and sensitive detection of free bilirubin in blood serum using human serum albumin stabilized gold nanoclusters as fluorometric and colorimetric probe
Fluorimetric detection of hypochlorite using albumin stabilized gold nanoclusters
Photoluminescent organosilane-functionalized carbon dots as temperature probes
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence
Localization of au nanoclusters on layered double hydroxides nanosheets: confinement-induced emission enhancement and temperature-responsive luminescence
pH-Sensitive gold nanoclusters: preparation and analytical applications for urea, urease, and urease inhibitor detection
Optical detection of glucose and acetylcholine esterase inhibitors by H2O2-sensitive CdSe/ZnS quantum dots
Fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate-conjugated protein-directed synthesis of gold nanoclusters for fluorescent ratiometric sensing of an enzyme–substrate system
Small molecule-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for cations anions, and biomolecules
A highly selective and sensitive ratiometric chemodosimeter for Hg2+ ions based on an iridium(III) complex via thioacetal deprotection reaction
Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on gold nanoclusters and alizarin red-boronic acid for monitoring glucose in brain microdialysate
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting hydrogen sulfide in aqueous media and inside live cells
Heterogeneous assembled nanocomplexes for ratiometric detection of highly reactive oxygen species in vitro and in vivo
Visualizing gaseous nitrogen dioxide by ratiometric fluorescence of carbon nanodots–quantum dots hybrid
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
One-pot synthesis of near-infrared fluorescent gold clusters for cellular fluorescence lifetime imaging
Synthesis, characterization, and bioconjugation of fluorescent gold nanoclusters toward biological labeling applications
Ratiometric fluorescence probe for monitoring hydroxyl radical in live cells based on gold nanoclusters
A mitochondria-localized two-photon fluorescent probe for ratiometric imaging of hydrogen peroxide in live tissue
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
An effective approach to enhanced energy-transfer efficiency from up-converting phosphors and increased assay sensitivity
Transient absorption study of peridinin and peridinin-chlorophyll a-protein after two-photon excitation
Quenching of photoluminescence in conjugates of quantum dots and single-walled carbon nanotube
Hydrogen peroxide sensitive hemoglobin-capped gold nanoclusters as a fluorescence enhancing sensor for the label-free detection of glucose
A dual-emission fluorescent nanocomplex of gold-cluster-decorated silica particles for live cell imaging of highly reactive oxygen species
Biomolecule-stabilized Au nanoclusters as a fluorescence probe for sensitive detection of glucose
Glutathione-protected gold clusters revisited: bridging the gap between gold(I)-thiolate complexes and thiolate-protected gold nanocrystals
In vivo nir fluorescence imaging, biodistribution, and toxicology of photoluminescent carbon dots produced from carbon nanotubes and graphite
In vivo renal clearance biodistribution, toxicity of gold nanoclusters

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zenghe Li had received his Ph.D. degree in Applied Chemistry from Beijing University of Chemical Technology in 2003. He is currently an Associate Professor of State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. His research is mainly focused on the preparation, functionalization and application of ultrafine particles.

Song Guo is currently a master student at Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, China. He is engaged in the synthesis, surface modification of carbon quantum dots, and potential application as fluorescence sensors.

Zhiqin Yuan obtained his Ph.D. degree from the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, in 2013 with Dr Yan He and Dr Edward S. Yeung. He is currently a school specially appointed Associate Professor of State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. His research interests are focused on synthesis and analytical application of metal nanoparticles and nanoclusters.

Chao Lu received his Ph.D. degree in Materials Science from Chinese Academy of Sciences. He is currently a full professor of State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. In 2011, he was selected to participate in the ‘New Century Outstanding Talent’ scheme of the Ministry of Education. His research interests are focused on the synthesis, characterization of nanostructured materials, nanosensors, and chemiluminescence. He is responsible of national and international research projects and has published more than 90 papers in peer−refereeing international journals.
